metadata.subject: Career Education
metadata.description: Career Education 8 introduces students to the process of self-awareness and career exploration. Students begin to identify their strengths, preferences, and values, explore how work contributes to community and society, and develop foundational skills in goal-setting, project management, and decision-making. First Peoples perspectives on work, community contribution, and traditional knowledge as careers are integrated throughout. This course is the beginning of the lifelong career planning process all BC students engage with through graduation.
metadata.general_description: An introductory career education course developing self-awareness, goal-setting, career exploration, community connection, and foundational workplace readiness skills — beginning the career planning journey toward graduation and beyond.

big_ideas.statement: Reflecting on our preferences and skills helps us identify the steps we need to take to achieve our career goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Self-knowledge is the foundation of career planning. By honestly reflecting on what they enjoy, what they are good at, and what matters to them, students can begin to chart meaningful pathways toward fulfilling futures.

big_ideas.statement: The value of work in our lives, communities, and society can be viewed from diverse perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Work is not just paid employment — it includes unpaid care work, volunteering, creative pursuits, and traditional practices. Different cultures and communities value different kinds of contribution, and these perspectives shape how we understand a meaningful working life.

big_ideas.statement: Achieving our learning goals requires effort and perseverance.
big_ideas.elaboration: Growth — whether academic, personal, or professional — requires sustained effort and the resilience to persist through challenges and setbacks. A growth mindset is a foundational career competency.

big_ideas.statement: Adapting to economic and labour market changes requires flexibility.
big_ideas.elaboration: The world of work is constantly evolving — shaped by technology, globalization, and shifting social needs. Developing flexibility, curiosity, and the capacity to learn new skills is essential preparation for a rapidly changing labour market.

big_ideas.statement: Our career paths reflect the personal, community, and educational choices we make.
big_ideas.elaboration: Career paths are not predetermined — they are shaped by the choices students make about their learning, relationships, and values. Understanding this agency empowers students to make intentional, informed decisions.

competencies.raw: Use self-assessment and reflection to develop awareness of their strengths, preferences, and skills; recognize the impact of personal public identity; demonstrate respect, collaboration, and inclusivity when solving problems with others; set and achieve realistic learning goals with perseverance and resilience.
i_can_statements: I can use self-assessment tools and reflection to identify my strengths, preferences, and skills.
I can recognize that my online presence and public identity have real impacts on my current and future opportunities.
I can demonstrate respect, collaboration, and inclusivity when working with diverse groups.
I can set realistic learning goals and show perseverance and resilience in working toward them.

competencies.raw: Question how individual purposes and passions address local and global community needs; explore diverse perspectives on how work contributes to community and society; appreciate networks of resources and mentors in career exploration; apply research skills to explore diverse career possibilities and career clusters.
i_can_statements: I can explore how my own interests and passions connect to real needs in my community and the wider world.
I can investigate how different types of work — paid, unpaid, traditional — contribute to community and society.
I can identify and appreciate mentors, role models, and community networks that can support my career exploration.
I can use research skills to explore diverse career possibilities and understand career clusters.

competencies.raw: Recognize how curriculum and co-curricular activities influence career paths; question family expectations and community needs in career decisions; explore volunteer opportunities that stimulate entrepreneurial and innovative thinking; apply decision-making strategies to life, work, or community problems; demonstrate safety skills and appreciate workplace safety.
i_can_statements: I can recognize how my course choices and extracurricular activities shape my future options.
I can thoughtfully consider family expectations and community needs in my career decision-making.
I can explore volunteering and community involvement as meaningful ways to develop skills and clarify interests.
I can apply decision-making strategies to navigate choices in my learning and life.
I can identify workplace hazards and demonstrate basic workplace safety knowledge and practices.

areas.name: Personal Development
areas.raw: Goal-setting strategies, self-assessment, reflection, and project management.
topics: Goal-setting: SMART goals — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Self-assessment tools: interest inventories, skills checklists, and values clarification
Strengths identification: personal assets, achievements, and areas for growth
Reflection practices: journals, exit tickets, and portfolio documentation
Project management basics: planning, timelines, resource allocation, and review
Growth mindset: understanding effort, challenge, and learning from mistakes
Personal values: identifying what matters most and how values guide choices

areas.name: Connections to Community
areas.raw: Local and global needs, cultural awareness, factors affecting job types, volunteering.
topics: Types of work: paid employment, self-employment, volunteering, and traditional practices
Career clusters: the broad categories of work (e.g., health, trades, technology, arts, social services)
Local and global community needs: how careers respond to social and environmental challenges
Cultural and social awareness: diverse perspectives on work, success, and contribution
First Peoples perspectives on work: traditional knowledge keepers, land stewardship, and cultural practices as careers
Factors affecting job availability: geography, economy, and community context
Volunteering: exploring interests, building skills, and contributing to community

areas.name: Life and Career Plan
areas.raw: Graduation requirements, mentors, technology, workplace safety.
topics: BC graduation requirements: credits, Dogwood requirements, and grad transitions
Secondary school planning: course selection and its influence on future pathways
Mentors, role models, and networks: identifying and building relationships that support growth
Workplace safety basics: hazard identification, WHMIS symbols, and worker rights and responsibilities
Technology's influence on learning and work: digital tools, online learning, and evolving job skills
Digital footprint and public identity: managing online presence responsibly
Introduction to entrepreneurship: creativity, risk-taking, and problem-solving
